dialogues.text: I've been up since four. Couldn't settle — I kept thinking about you, the way you were as a boy. Here. Take this. Please, don't argue with an old woman before breakfast.
dialogues.yes_response: Good. You've always been too proud to take help. Don't you start being proud with me now, of all days.
dialogues.no_response: I'll leave it here on the table, by the fruit. It'll be waiting when you're ready for it.

dialogues.text: I sold your grandfather's watch. The good one, from the drawer. Don't make that face — the money should be yours now. He'd have wanted it walking around in your pocket, not stopped in a drawer.
dialogues.yes_response: He would have been proud of you. He never said it the way he should have. Neither did I, come to that.
dialogues.no_response: Then I'll put it in an envelope with your name. You come back for it whenever the day lets you.

dialogues.text: I made far too much soup again — I always do, it's a habit from when the house was full. And I've this set aside for you besides. Take both, you'll do me the favor.
dialogues.yes_response: You look pale, my love. You've looked pale for weeks now. Come inside and sit soon. Promise me soon.
dialogues.no_response: The soup will keep. So will I, more or less. Come back when you can manage it.

dialogues.text: I lit a candle for you every morning this week, before the church even unlocked the side door. The verger knows me now. And I brought you this, while I'm at it.
dialogues.yes_response: I say a prayer over your name every day. I'm not going to stop just because you're grown and out the door.
dialogues.no_response: That's alright. I'll keep the prayers going anyway. Those you can't say no to, you'll notice.

dialogues.text: Your aunt rang, asking after you. I didn't rightly know what to tell her, so I told her very little. Here — just take this, and don't make me lie better next time.
dialogues.yes_response: I told her you were doing fine. I do hope that was the right thing to put in her ear. It felt kind, anyway.
dialogues.no_response: I'll tell her you sent your love. That's true enough, and it's all she really wanted to hear.

dialogues.text: I don't need much. Never have, even when I thought I did. You take this off my hands. I mean it — it's no use sitting in a tin under my bed.
dialogues.yes_response: Good. That's all I wanted, really. Just to do something with these hands besides wring them.
dialogues.no_response: Stubborn. You get that from your father, the whole stubborn streak of it. Come back soon anyway.

dialogues.text: I keep wanting to give you things. I can't entirely explain it — it's come over me strong this past while. So. Here. Let me.
dialogues.yes_response: I just need to feel useful to you. You understand. You've always understood me without the long explanation.
dialogues.no_response: Come back soon. That's the only thing I really want from you. The rest is just my hands needing a job.

dialogues.text: I found some money I'd clean forgotten about — folded in the pocket of an old winter coat. I take that as a sign it was always meant for you.
dialogues.yes_response: Sometimes things turn up just when a person needs them. I like to believe that. It costs nothing to believe.
dialogues.no_response: It's not going anywhere, and neither am I. The coat'll hold it a while longer.

dialogues.text: I baked this morning. First time in months — the oven and I had fallen out. I couldn't tell you why today of all days. Take this too, with the bread.
dialogues.yes_response: You're too thin, my love. I notice these things. Mothers and grandmothers, we always notice the thinness.
dialogues.no_response: The door is always open. You know that. It's always open, day or night, you've only to come through it.

dialogues.text: Before you go. Take this. And — I love you. You know that, don't you. I worry I leaned on showing it and forgot to say it plainly.
dialogues.yes_response: Good. Just — come back. That's the whole of what I'm asking. Just come back through that door.
dialogues.no_response: I know. I know you do. Just come back tomorrow, then. Tomorrow will do me fine.
